unit.name: Direct Preference Optimization
unit.description: Derives DPO as a direct preference-pair objective that avoids the RL loop.
unit.summary: The instructor introduces DPO because full RLHF is complex, sampling-heavy, hyperparameter-sensitive, and historically limited to high-resource groups [ts=3362s] [ts=3413s]. The key idea is to write the reward model in terms of the language model: if probability mass is shifted toward completions humans like, model log probabilities can correspond to human preference [ts=3490s] [ts=3528s]. DPO is possible because after preference labels are collected, the reward-model fitting and RL optimization steps add no new external information [ts=3561s] [ts=3590s]. The derivation starts from the KL-regularized expected-reward objective and gives a closed-form Boltzmann-style optimum: reweight the reference model by exponentiated reward, normalized by a partition function Z(x) [ts=3614s] [ts=3711s]. Z(x) is intractable because it would require summing over all possible completions, but algebra expresses reward using a beta-scaled log probability ratio between the optimized policy and reference model [ts=3721s] [ts=3792s]. When plugged into the Bradley-Terry objective, the partition function cancels because winning and losing completions share the same input x [ts=3953s] [ts=4009s]. The result is a direct binary-classification-style loss over preferred versus dispreferred answers, with no RL loop [ts=4057s] [ts=4089s].

key_points.text: DPO is motivated as a simpler alternative to complex RLHF.
DPO writes the reward model in terms of language-model probabilities.
The KL-regularized objective has a closed-form optimum that reweights the reference model by reward.
The partition function Z(x) is intractable because it sums over all possible completions.
Reward can be expressed as a beta-scaled log-ratio between optimized and reference policies.
The partition function cancels in Bradley-Terry reward differences for the same input.
DPO becomes a binary classification loss over preference pairs.

The key idea is to write the reward model in terms of the language model: if probability mass is shifted toward completions humans like, model log probabilities can correspond to human preference [ts=3490s] [ts=3528s]. DPO is possible because after preference labels are collected, the reward-model fitting and RL optimization steps add no new external information [ts=3561s] [ts=3590s]. The derivation starts from the KL-regularized expected-reward objective and gives a closed-form Boltzmann-style optimum: reweight the reference model by exponentiated reward, normalized by a partition function Z(x) [ts=3614s] [ts=3711s]. Z(x) is intractable because it would require summing over all possible completions, but algebra expresses reward using a beta-scaled log probability ratio between the optimized policy and reference model [ts=3721s] [ts=3792s]. When plugged into the Bradley-Terry objective, the partition function cancels because winning and losing completions share the same input x [ts=3953s] [ts=4009s]. The result is a direct binary-classification-style loss over preferred versus dispreferred answers, with no RL loop [ts=4057s] [ts=4089s].

kp_catalog.name: Direct Preference Optimization
kp_catalog.description: A direct classification-style preference objective that avoids a separate RLHF optimization loop. DPO as a simpler preference-optimization method that avoids full PPO-style RL infrastructure.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because direct Preference Optimization formalizes how a direct classification-style preference objective that avoids a separate RLHF optimization loop, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though roles split between capstone and gateway.

kp_catalog.name: Reward modeling from pairwise preferences
kp_catalog.description: Turning pairwise human preferences into a reward-model objective based on reward differences. Pairwise reward-model training and RewardBench as local evaluation for reward models.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward modeling from pairwise preferences formalizes how turning pairwise human preferences into a reward-model objective based on reward differences, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Reward hacking and KL regularization
kp_catalog.description: The risk of exploiting learned rewards and the KL-style penalty that keeps the policy near the reference model. Optimizers exploiting incomplete reward specifications in reward-model-based alignment.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward hacking and KL regularization formalizes how the risk of exploiting learned rewards and the KL-style penalty that keeps the policy near the reference model, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
